cataloging_subject.label: Life of Saint Brendan
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Life of Saint Brendan transmitted as part of the South English Legendary. CME00107 represents Carl Horstmann's 1874 witness-based edition: a running text from Bodleian MS Ashmole 43, ff. 71v-80v, with variant material associated with British Library Harley MS 2277, Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 108, and Bodleian MS Bodley 779. Horstmann's German headnote and textual apparatus are modeled as an editorial content part, not as a second medieval work. The record does not describe the whole South English Legendary, every one of the seventeen DIMEV witnesses, Thomas Wright's prose Brendan, or the physical codices as if they were the work itself.

catalog_summary.title: Life of Saint Brendan
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
catalog_summary.date: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Horstmann edition published 1874
catalog_summary.region: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established

titles.supplied_by: modern cataloging title used by the Bodleian catalog and aligned with DIMEV

assertion.notes: A supplied work title, not a claim that every witness carries this exact heading.

titles.value: Die altenglische Legende von St. Brendan aus Ms. Ashmol. 43, fol. 71b

scope.description: The 1874 edited representation rather than a medieval title

titles.value: Saint Brendan the holy man was here of our land

assertion.notes: DIMEV's normalized indexing form; witness spellings vary.

titles.supplied_by: Bodleian catalog transcription of the Ashmole rubric

assertion.notes: A witness rubric, not adopted as the preferred uniform title.

summaries.value: A Middle English verse life recounting Brendan's sacred sea voyage with a company of monks. The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.

CME00107 contains a 733-line main poem encoded in one principal verse group, followed by serialized editorial apparatus: a German headnote, 156 notes in the complete file, four verse lines embedded in the Harley variants, and separate Harley, Laud, and Bodley variant divisions. The printed source spans pp. 17-48.

The work is represented through Horstmann's Ashmole-based edition. Ashmole 43 is the base witness; Laud Misc. 108 and Bodley 779 are directly labeled variant witnesses; Harley 2277 reaches the securely supported lineage through Wright's 1844 printed transcription because Horstmann explicitly cites Wright, while Horstmann's own direct consultation of Harley remains unproved. DIMEV lists thirteen additional witnesses that are relevant to the wider textual tradition but are not immediate sources of CME00107.

Work, witness, edition, and encoding layers are kept separate. The thirteenth-century composition range belongs to the abstract poem; dates, regions, and dialects assigned to individual codices describe those witnesses; 1874 describes Horstmann's publication; and XML structural counts describe the digital representation. The work-level record therefore does not inherit Gloucestershire, Somerset, Hampshire, or Oxford as a precise place of composition.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
notes: The identity is unknown. Saint Brendan is the subject of the legend, not its author.
The poem probably adapts the Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani tradition, but no physical exemplar or named translator is established.

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor, base-text transcriber, and collator for the 1874 representation encoded as CME00107.

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is supported by the evidence inspected.

scope.description: Probable adaptation of Latin Navigatio material into the South English Legendary form

assertion.notes: The precise Latin recension, exemplar, and mechanics of adaptation remain unresolved.

scope.description: The 1874 edited representation, including base text and apparatus
Readings associated with Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779

assertion.notes: The Harley route is qualified in the lineage record: Wright's 1844 printed transcription is the explicit supported intermediary, and direct consultation of Harley by Horstmann remains unresolved.
A specialist composition range for the abstract poem; it is not inferred from the date of any one surviving witness.

date_statements.display: Extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century in Laud Misc. 108

scope.description: Current earliest attestation among the immediate witnesses researched

assertion.notes: The codex's broader 1290-1400 history includes later additions; the target belongs to its early core.

scope.description: Publication of the immediate source edition, not the first circulation of the medieval work

places.label: England
notes: Used only for the work's broad cultural origin.

places.label: Southern England
notes: A deliberately broad language-region label; PCMEP says the original dialect is unknown but rules out northern origin.

assertion.notes: The work is Middle English and part of the South English Legendary tradition; a more precise place of composition is not established.

scope.description: Reconstructed original language, not any one witness dialect

assertion.notes: PCMEP reports that the original dialect is unknown, though northern origin can be ruled out; Gloucestershire, Somerset, and Hampshire assignments belong to particular witnesses.

scope.description: Work-level form; the separately modeled editorial apparatus does not make the medieval work mixed

form_statements.description: A Middle English poem in rhyming aa couplets, commonly described as septenary with seven stresses and two half-lines.
German prose framing and variant notation with four Harley readings encoded as verse lines.

assertion.notes: A descriptive narrative mode within the saints' life; not used here to reclassify the poem as secular romance.
Lavery emphasizes the plain vernacular didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary adaptation.

subject_statements.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

related_works.title: South English Legendary
notes: The Life circulates as one component of a fluid multi-witness saints' legend compilation; the component order and textual state vary among codices.

related_works.title: Navigatio Sancti Brendani
notes: Lavery argues that the Latin Navigatio is probably the poem's principal source. This is a work-level relationship; no physical exemplar or precise recension is identified.

related_works.title: Voyage of Saint Brendan by Benedeit
notes: Part of the broader Brendan voyage tradition, but Lavery rejects direct use of the Anglo-Norman poem as a secondary source for the South English Legendary version.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Brendan
content_parts.summary: The anonymous Middle English verse saints' life, represented by Horstmann's Ashmole 43 running text.
notes: The 733 count is the main XML stream. It is not silently reconciled with DIMEV's 738, Lavery's 734, or PCMEP's 732 plus final Amen.
Harley, Laud, and Bodley readings are source apparatus for this work, not additional content works.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's headnote and textual apparatus
content_parts.summary: German editorial framing, embedded notes, and witness-specific readings associated with Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779.

locators.value: DIV1 direct P plus NOTE elements and three DIV2 variant divisions
ff. 41v-51r, represented through Wright's printed metrical text unless direct Horstmann consultation is established

notes: The three item count refers to the three named variant divisions, not the total number of individual variant readings.
The printed article interleaves Harley variants with the running poem; the XML serializes them after the main verse group.

extent.description: The cataloged unit contains one medieval verse work plus one editorial-apparatus part. The XML has one DIV1, three DIV2 elements, four HEAD elements, two LG elements, 737 total L elements, 733 main-work L elements, four Harley-apparatus L elements, 156 NOTE elements, 53 PB elements, 164 MILESTONE elements, 28 P elements, and 1,081 HI elements. Total XML lines include apparatus and therefore are not a work-level line count. DIMEV reports 738, Lavery 734, and PCMEP 733 as 732 plus Amen; those measures remain explicitly unresolved.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00107.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; EEBO/IDG; EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1, LG, NOTE, and DIV2
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for item identifiers, Horstmann title and editor, UMDL handle, Middle English and German layers, the complete encoded work and apparatus, printed-page milestones, narrative contents, and structural boundaries.

evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, P, and HI counts and nesting
evidence.summary: The file has one DIV1, three DIV2 elements, four HEAD elements, two LG elements, 737 total L elements, 733 main-work L elements, four Harley-apparatus L elements, 156 NOTE elements, 53 PB elements, 164 MILESTONE elements, 28 P elements, and 1,081 HI elements. The main LG contains 733 lines; the second LG of four lines occurs in the Harley apparatus.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die altenglische Legende von St. Brendan aus Ms. Ashmol. 43, fol. 71b,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 53 (1874): 17-48.
evidence.summary: Primary source for the immediate edition's title, date, editor, Ashmole base witness, Harley/Wright statement, Laud and Bodley apparatus, page boundaries, editorial presentation, and continuation notice.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 4567, IMEV/NIMEV 2868, version 1.0 data release.
evidence.locator: record 4567 and seventeen linked witness entries
evidence.summary: Supports the normalized incipit, title, DIMEV and IMEV identifiers, saints' life subject, aa-couplet form, a 738-line catalog extent, and a seventeen-witness census including Horstmann's four sources.

evidence.citation: PCMEP, text details for Saint Brendan.
evidence.locator: composition, dialect, verse form, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, line count, and Harley witness entry
evidence.summary: Supports the 1250-1290 composition range with circa 1260 suggested, a broad southern rather than northern original dialect, septenary aa-couplet form, a 733-line count described as 732 lines plus final Amen, and Harley ff. 41v-51r.

evidence.citation: Simon Lavery, 'The Source of the St Brendan Story in the South English Legendary,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 15 (1984): 21-32.
evidence.summary: Supports probable principal dependence on the Latin Navigatio, rejection of direct dependence on Benedeit's Anglo-Norman Voyage, and the poem's abridged vernacular didactic/homiletic hagiographic character; Lavery reports 734 lines.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Ashmole 43.
evidence.summary: Supports the Ashmole work title, rubric, folio boundary, incipit and explicit, witness date and localization, and base-witness distinction.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 2277, record 040-002048108.
evidence.locator: South English Legendary, ff. 1r-232v; target at ff. 41v-51r established by PCMEP and DIMEV
evidence.summary: Supports the Harley witness identity, circa-1300 date, South-West English production, material and script. Its witness properties are not transferred to the abstract work's place or date.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.summary: Supports the surviving Laud boundary, rubric, lacuna from two lost leaves, and the target's position in the manuscript's early core; used cautiously as an early attestation rather than a composition date.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Bodley 779.
evidence.locator: Life of St Brendan, ff. 1r-1v and 3r-10r
evidence.summary: Supports the discontiguous Bodley boundary, false Saint Patrick rubric and corrected Brendan incipit, witness date, and Hampshire orthographic localization; those witness properties are not transferred to the abstract work.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00107.
evidence.locator: entities, relations, access, rights, editorial_practices, evidence, and open_questions
evidence.summary: Binds the work metadata to the repository XML, U-M encoding, Horstmann edition, Wright intermediary, four manuscript witnesses, three complete Oxford facsimile sequences, DIMEV census, and Lavery source study without conflating their layers.

open_questions.question: How should the 733 main XML lines, DIMEV's 738 lines, Lavery's 734 lines, and PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen be reconciled across witnesses and editorial conventions?
next_steps: Collate lineation in Horstmann, Wright, the four manuscripts, and a current critical edition.
Record separately whether Amen, rubrics, split lines, lacunae, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Retain 733 only as the present main-stream XML count until reconciled.

open_questions.question: Which Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani recension or exemplar most directly underlies the South English Legendary adaptation?
next_steps: Consult scholarship published after Lavery's 1984 study.
Compare distinctive narrative and verbal readings against the Latin recension tradition.
Keep probable work-level adaptation separate from any claim about a physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's place and dialect of composition be localized more closely than broadly southern England without transferring properties from later witnesses?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic and South English Legendary scholarship on reconstructed archetypal language.
Separate original-language claims from the Gloucestershire, Somerset, and Hampshire profiles of individual witnesses.
Retain England as the broad cultural origin and southern England as tentative only.

open_questions.question: Did Horstmann consult Harley MS 2277 directly, or is every Harley reading in CME00107 mediated by Wright's 1844 transcription?
next_steps: Compare Horstmann's Harley apparatus against Wright once manuscript images are again accessible.
Search Horstmann's methodological statements, correspondence, and working papers.
Retain the Wright intermediary and do not state direct consultation until evidenced.

notes: The preferred title is a modern supplied work title; witness rubrics and Horstmann's German article title remain separately identified.
Saint Brendan is the subject, not an attributed author. The medieval author or adapter is anonymous.
German belongs to Horstmann's retained editorial framing and remains described in the content boundary; it is not exposed as an intrinsic language or genre of the medieval work.
The main work is verse. The record's mixed editorial-apparatus part does not change the work-level form to mixed.
Romance is not assigned as a primary genre: the voyage contains adventure and marvels, but the strongest grounded classification is hagiographic saints' legend and religious poetry.

All three Oxford facsimiles are complete manuscript sequences locally cached and precisely mapped to work folios; their image rights are CC BY-NC 4.0 and do not authorize for-profit facsimile redistribution.
The record remains partial because the line-count conventions, Latin source recension, original linguistic localization, and exact Harley collation route remain unresolved.
